title: Why women are taught to apologise for wanting
subtitle: An exploration of where the guilt around female financial desire comes from, and why it is not yours to carry.
excerpt: A long-form reading on the cultural conditioning that teaches women to edit down their financial desires, apologise for ambition, and frame wealth-wanting as selfishness. Part of Week 3 of the MONEY library.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Synthesised from Rebecca J Page's MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (2025), themes in sociological literature on women and money, and public-domain analysis of gender and financial socialisation.

content.text: Most women who do this kind of work arrive with a specific flavour of guilt around money. Not the basic fear of running out, something more particular. The guilt of wanting too much. The discomfort of naming a number that feels excessive. The habit of framing financial desire in terms of other people (I want security for my children; I want to be generous; I want to be able to give back) rather than simply: I want this for me.
That edit is not random. It is taught.
The caring framing

From an early age, girls receive a consistent message: your desires are acceptable when they are framed as care for others. Ambition is fine when it is selfless. Wanting money is fine when it is for security, for family, for generosity. The minute the want becomes personal (the expensive coat, the larger house, the bigger number), it starts to feel harder to justify.

This is not a universal human pattern. It is a gender-specific one. Studies in financial socialisation consistently find that girls are more likely than boys to be taught to save, to be cautious, to think of money as security rather than as a tool for growth. Boys are more likely to be encouraged to take risks, to aim for returns, to think about wealth as accumulation rather than as a buffer against disaster.
The effect is that many women arrive in adulthood with a broken relationship with money and with wanting itself. The guilt runs deeper than the money. It is the desire itself.
What greedy actually means

"Greedy" is one of the words most reliably attached to women who express financial ambition without immediately wrapping it in altruism. Ask a man in a negotiation what he wants, and he will tell you. Ask a woman the same question, and she will often qualify it, explain why she needs it, establish that she has earned it, frame it in terms of what it will let her do for others.

The apology for wanting is so deeply installed that most women don't notice they are doing it. It shows up as shrinking a number in a salary negotiation. As pricing a service lower than you know it's worth. As wincing when someone asks what you charge. As adding "is that okay?" to a quote.

None of this is a character flaw. It is a conditioned response, one that made social sense in environments where women's access to money was contingent on pleasing others, and where expressing financial ambition attracted penalty rather than reward.
The inheritance of editors

The most direct source of the edit is almost always the women immediately ahead of you. Not because those women were wrong or damaged, but because they were passing on the safest available strategy for their circumstances.

A grandmother who never controlled her own finances learned not to want loudly. A mother who was financially dependent learned to justify every expenditure. An aunt who was told she was too ambitious edited down her expectations to match what was available to her. They handed the edit forward not as a lesson but as survival.

The women in your line were not wrong to adapt. The conditions they adapted to were the problem. And those conditions have changed enough (in most places, for most women with the resources to be reading this) that the edit is no longer load-bearing. It no longer keeps you safe. It just keeps you small.
Releasing the edit
Releasing the guilt around wanting is not about deciding to be selfish. It is about examining whether "selfish" was ever the right word for what you were feeling.

Wanting a good income is not selfish. Wanting financial security is not greedy. Wanting to build real, lasting, generational wealth is not excess. These are reasonable desires for a person who knows that money is a tool, and that more of it means more freedom, more choice, more ability to act on what matters.
The work of Day 15 is not to overcome a character defect. It is to set down a learned response that was never yours to carry in the first place.

When you do the Day 15 tapping, you are not fixing something broken inside you. You are releasing a protection mechanism built for a world that no longer exists in the same shape. That is different. And it is worth knowing the difference.
Where this comes from
Original to homemade.education. Drawn from Rebecca J Page's MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program and research on women and money. Full reading list in the Sources block.
